title: The tidy spreadsheet versus the unknown vendor
scenario: A committee quickly rejects a new vendor mainly because its performance range is less well specified, even though the incumbent vendor's documented failure rate is high and the committee barely compares the expected upside.
moveOptions: List the known risks, unknown risks, and plausible payoffs for both vendors before the cleaner-looking option gets privileged.
Keep the incumbent because ambiguous options are automatically more dangerous.
Ignore the uncertainty question and decide by who spoke most confidently in the meeting.
Choose whichever option has the longer slide deck.
correctMove: List the known risks, unknown risks, and plausible payoffs for both vendors before the cleaner-looking option gets privileged.
biasExplanation: The weaker-specified option is being penalized mainly for ambiguity itself rather than for a balanced comparison of expected value.
moveExplanation: Symmetric comparison is the first repair because the cleaner presentation should not be allowed to impersonate the better option.

title: The conclusion sounds right, so the argument must be fine
scenario: A student nods along with a weak argument because the conclusion matches what she already thinks is obviously true, and only later notices that the premises did not actually support it well.
moveOptions: Hold the conclusion fixed for a moment and evaluate whether the inferential structure would still look strong if the ending were unwelcome.
Trust the argument because true conclusions usually come from solid reasoning.
Grade the argument by how morally attractive the conclusion feels.
Ignore the premises because the main point has already landed.
correctMove: Hold the conclusion fixed for a moment and evaluate whether the inferential structure would still look strong if the ending were unwelcome.
biasExplanation: The believability of the conclusion is inflating the perceived quality of the reasoning.
moveExplanation: The clean repair is to separate structure from conclusion approval and see whether the same form still persuades when the ending loses its emotional advantage.

title: The expert skipped the missing steps
scenario: A senior engineer explains a workflow to new teammates, then gets irritated when they still look lost because the explanation felt perfectly obvious to him as he gave it.
moveOptions: Rebuild the explanation from the first missing step the new teammates do not yet have rather than from the expert's finished map.
Tell the team to pay closer attention because the explanation was already simple enough.
Add more jargon so the explanation sounds more precise.
Judge the explanation by whether it felt smooth to the speaker.
correctMove: Rebuild the explanation from the first missing step the new teammates do not yet have rather than from the expert's finished map.
biasExplanation: The engineer is underestimating the audience's perspective gap because his own completed understanding makes the sequence feel shorter than it is.
moveExplanation: Restarting from the audience's missing context is the most direct correction for a knowledge-compressed explanation.

title: Now that it is ours, it feels worth much more
scenario: A team receives a tool through a merger and quickly starts demanding a much higher price to give it up than they ever would have proposed paying to acquire it from scratch.
moveOptions: Ask what the team would honestly pay for the same tool if it were not already in their possession.
Treat current ownership as proof that the tool has already earned a premium.
Ignore price and decide by which team feels more attached.
Assume inherited assets are always undervalued by outsiders.
correctMove: Ask what the team would honestly pay for the same tool if it were not already in their possession.
biasExplanation: Possession itself is inflating valuation, not just the tool's underlying utility.
moveExplanation: An outside-view willingness-to-pay question strips away some of the valuation boost created by ownership alone.

title: He must have meant that as a slight
scenario: A colleague sends a short reply with no greeting, and another teammate immediately starts interpreting the message as deliberate disrespect without seriously considering time pressure or tone-loss in text.
moveOptions: List at least two non-hostile explanations for the same message before deciding what level of response is warranted.
Assume the most threatening motive because it is safer never to be naive.
Reply with matching hostility so the other person learns the boundary quickly.
Use the shortest possible message as evidence of hidden aggression by default.
correctMove: List at least two non-hostile explanations for the same message before deciding what level of response is warranted.
biasExplanation: An ambiguous act is being promoted too quickly into a hostile-intent reading.
moveExplanation: The fastest repair is to reopen motive space before retaliation hardens around the first threatening interpretation.

title: It just feels more trustworthy now
scenario: A proposal begins winning support after people have seen the slogan and design treatment repeatedly for weeks, even though the substantive arguments have not improved much.
moveOptions: Ask what reasons for preferring the proposal remain if its familiarity advantage is mentally removed from the comparison.
Treat repetition as proof that the proposal has earned its support on the merits.
Increase the repetition even more because familiarity and quality are basically the same thing.
Ignore all newer alternatives because the known option now feels smoother.
correctMove: Ask what reasons for preferring the proposal remain if its familiarity advantage is mentally removed from the comparison.
biasExplanation: Repeated exposure is increasing liking and trust beyond what the evidential change alone would justify.
moveExplanation: Removing the repetition advantage conceptually helps test whether the proposal is winning on reasons or on fluency.

title: The possibility is so scary that the percentage barely matters
scenario: A parent treats two very different low-probability risks almost the same because once the feared outcome is imagined vividly enough, the exact probabilities stop changing the reaction much.
moveOptions: Write down both the magnitude and the explicit probability for each risk before deciding how much weight each one deserves.
Ignore the percentages because emotionally serious possibilities should all receive the same response.
Judge the risks only by which image is hardest to shake.
Assume the most vivid danger is also the most likely one.
correctMove: Write down both the magnitude and the explicit probability for each risk before deciding how much weight each one deserves.
biasExplanation: The vivid scenario is overwhelming the probability information that should differentiate the choices.
moveExplanation: Putting magnitude and probability back on the same table is the first correction when possibility has started impersonating plausibility.

title: That only happens to other teams
scenario: A leadership group keeps delaying contingency planning because the disruptive scenario feels too exceptional and too unlikely to happen in their own case, despite similar failures in comparable organizations.
moveOptions: Use a precommitted escalation threshold based on warning signs instead of waiting for the threat to feel normal enough to plan around.
Wait until the unusual scenario feels more intuitive before acting on it.
Ignore the comparable failures because this organization has a stronger culture.
Treat contingency planning as alarmist unless the failure has already started.
correctMove: Use a precommitted escalation threshold based on warning signs instead of waiting for the threat to feel normal enough to plan around.
biasExplanation: The familiar baseline is outranking the warning signs because the disruptive scenario still feels too unlike ordinary life.
moveExplanation: Precommitted thresholds reduce reliance on whether the unusual threat feels emotionally real in time.

title: Now I want the opposite just because you pushed
scenario: A teenager becomes more determined to reject a reasonable curfew after the parent frames the demand in a highly controlling way, even though the curfew itself did not initially seem unreasonable.
moveOptions: Separate the style of pressure from the substance of the curfew and ask what verdict you would reach if the same rule were proposed without the coercive tone.
Treat the urge to resist as proof that the rule must be bad.
Escalate the conflict so the autonomy issue fully overrides the practical issue.
Assume any externally imposed rule is automatically less rational than a self-chosen one.
correctMove: Separate the style of pressure from the substance of the curfew and ask what verdict you would reach if the same rule were proposed without the coercive tone.
biasExplanation: The perceived threat to freedom is now doing more motivational work than the underlying merits of the option.
moveExplanation: Decoupling the pressure style from the option itself is the cleanest way to see whether the resistance is about autonomy signaling or about the rule's actual quality.

title: The survey answer that sounds right in public
scenario: Employees report near-perfect adherence to a policy in a non-anonymous survey, but later anonymous measures and observed behavior suggest the original answers were much cleaner than reality.
moveOptions: Use anonymous or behavior-based measurement before treating the polished self-reports as a reliable read of actual behavior.
Assume the public answers are more truthful because people are less likely to joke when names are attached.
Take the most flattering answer as the best estimate of team culture.
Ignore the behavioral evidence because the survey looked professional.
correctMove: Use anonymous or behavior-based measurement before treating the polished self-reports as a reliable read of actual behavior.
biasExplanation: Audience pressure is shaping what people report about themselves more than the underlying behavior alone would support.
moveExplanation: Anonymity and behavior-based checks reduce image-management pressure and make the measurement less dependent on what sounds admirable in public.
